content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARKINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

SACRAMENTO                                591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000                    SAN FRANCISCO
770 L STREET, SUITE 800               MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941              650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814                                                      SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752              TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800                   TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800


                                        February 11, 1991


Jeanne Pritchard
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804-0807

Dear Ms. Pritchard:

     We are writing to request advice regarding certain duties of
our law firm as a lobbying firm, and also certain duties of our
attorneys and their spouses, under the lobbying provisions of the
Political Reform Act.  (Gov't Code § 86100 et seq.¹)  We file
quarterly reports as a lobbying firm, pursuant to sections
82038.5 and 86114.  Before this year, none of our attorneys or
their spouses have been appointed as members of state boards or
commissions.

     Our questions are set out below, along with what we have
found in our research on these questions.

                              Facts

     Attorneys, and spouses of attorneys in the firm, may be
appointed to state boards or commissions, thereby becoming public
officials.  They will sit on these boards or commissions on their
own volition, in their capacity as private citizens not as
employees of the firm.  The firm does not intend to lobby them,
or their agency, on behalf of any firm client.

     We understand that if an attorney or the spouse of an
attorney becomes a public official, he or she will disclose the
firm as a source of income on his or her Statement of Economic
Interests, if required by the provisions of the relevant conflict
of interest code (§ 87207).  In addition, we understand that if a
partner or the spouse of a partner becomes a public official and
the partner owns 10% or more of the firm, the public official
will also disclose the identity of all clients of the firm who
pay the firm fees rendering that partner's share $10,000 or more

_____________________
¹All further statutory references are to the Government Code.

Jeanne Pritchard
February 11, 1991
Page 2

($20,000 if the spouse is the public official). (§§ 82030,
87207(b)(2).)

In addition to receiving compensation, our attorneys
periodically attend firm-related functions, some of which entail
travel to and accommodation in other cities. Spouses sometimes
accompany attorneys to these functions. The firm usually pays
for both attorneys' and their spouses' expenses, including
travel, hotel and meal costs.

In addition to firm functions, clients sometimes initiate
events, inviting attorneys along with a guest of their choosing,
and then reimbursing the firm for the expenses of the invited
attorneys and their guests. In these instances, the client has
no concern whether or not attorneys actually bring their spouses,
usually does not care whether or not the spouses or the attorneys
are public officials, and has no matter before the public
official/attorney, public official/spouse, or his or her agency.

Questions

We request advice on the following questions:

1. Are we correct in interpreting the Roger Brown advice
letter (No. I-89-602 (11/28/89)) as stating that the firm may pay
for a public official/spouse's attendance at and travel to firm-
related events without violating section 86203 and without
incurring a reporting obligation under 86114?

2. Will paying for the expenses of the public
official/spouse at firm-related functions count toward the new
$250 annual gift limit?

3. Are the answers to questions #1 and #2 dependent on
whether the firm is registered to lobby the board or commission
on which the public official/spouse sits?

4. If an attorney and his or her spouse attend an event
initiated by a client, and if the client reimburses the firm for
the expenses of the public official/spouse, must the firm report
the transaction? Does the cost constitute a gift from the firm
to the public official/spouse? A gift from the client?

5. Is the answer to question #4 dependent on whether the
client is registered to lobby the board or commission on which
the public official/spouse sits?

6. Must the firm's public official/attorneys disclose the
costs of firm-related or client-initiated social events on their

Jeanne Pritchard
February 11, 1991
Page 3


Statements of Economic Interests? If disclosable, are the costs
properly characterized as "income" or "gifts"?

     7.     Must the firm report the salaries or other income of
our public official/attorneys on its lobbying firm report as an
activity expense or elsewhere?

                              Analysis

     Questions 1 to 3. Based on your previous advice to Roger
Brown (No. I-89-602 (11/28/89)), we understand that the
Commission's position is that the firm will not be making a
"gift" to the public official/spouse by paying for his or her
attendance at and travel to firm-related functions. According to
the Brown letter, the expense payment to the public
official/spouse will not violate section 86203 and will not be
reportable by the public official/spouse or the firm.2

     Questions 4 and 5. The conclusions of the Brown letter also
seem to apply to the situation in which a client invites the
firm's attorneys to a social event, tells them they may bring a
guest of their choosing, and then reimburses the firm for their
expenses. Even if an attorney brings a public official, neither
the firm nor the client intends the payment of the public
official's expenses to be a gift to the public official/spouse.
The Act's reporting and limitation rules therefore do not seem to
apply.

_________________________
     2The Brown letter concerned a law firm (which also was a
lobbying firm) which had several employees whose spouses were
public officials. You advised the firm that its employees could
bring their spouses who were public officials to firm events,
without violating the ten dollar gift limit, as long as the firm
neither "invited, suggested nor earmarked an invitation" for the
spouses. (See also Advice Letter No. A-85-126 to Gordon Duffy
(6/27/85).) In addition, you advised the firm that the payments
were not disclosable on the firm's lobbying firm report as long
as the firm did not intend them to be gifts to the public
officials/spouses.

     The facts of our situation appear very similar to the facts
of the Brown letter. Our firm does not require attorneys to
bring spouses to firm-related events but does indicate that they
may bring a guest of their choosing. In addition, when the firm
pays the spouse's expenses and travel costs to a firm-related
event, it does not intend the payment to be a gift to a public
official since the spouse's public appointment is irrelevant to
the firm.

Jeanne Pritchard
February 11, 1991
Page 4


Question 6. Your previous advice to Daniel Pellissier (No.
A-87-280 (12/8/87)) suggests that attorneys who become public
officials will not be required to disclose the costs of firm-
related or client-initiated events on their Statements of
Economic Interests. The Pellissier letter concluded that
". . . in computing 'income,' officials are not required to
include health benefits and other routine employment benefits
which are provided by the employer. Lunches, parties and other social events seem to fall within this
exception to the definition of income -- because they are
"routine" and because they are provided, in different degrees, to
all employees. The letter also concluded that the public benefit
from reporting routine fringe benefits is outweighed by the
significant burden the reportage places on filers. This
rationale for nondisclosure applies equally to public officials
reporting the costs of meals and parties provided through their
employment.

Question 7. In the Morrissey Opinion (2 FPPC Opns. 84
(7/6/76)), the Commission concluded that a lobbyist employer need
not disclose the salary of an employee who sits on an advisory
committee of a local agency as an "other payment to influence
administrative or legislative action" on its lobbyist employer
report. The Commission based its conclusion on the fact that the
employee did not sit the committee to serve the interest of
his employer. (2 FPPC Opns. at p. 89.)

Based on the Morrissey opinion, you advised Pillsbury,
Madison & Sutro, a law firm with reporting obligations as a
lobbying firm, that it must disclose on its lobbying firm report
payments it receives from clients for work one of its attorneys
does as a member of a state commission. (Advice Letter No.
A-86-284 to Teresa Craigie (10/31/86).) Because the firm billed
its clients for the attorney's time on the commission, you
concluded that he was acting to represent the interests of the
client and the firm.

In our case, the status of our attorneys and/or their
spouses who may be appointed to boards or commissions resembles
the status of the employee in the Morrissey opinion and is
distinguishable from the attorney in the Craigie advice letter.
As stated above, our attorneys will decide whether to accept
appointments on their own, will serve in their capacity as
private citizens, and will not represent the interests of the
firm or its clients. Also, the firm will not bill any of its
clients for attorneys' work on boards or commissions. The

Jeanne Pritchard
February 11, 1991
Page 5


salaries of the public officials/attorneys therefore seem
nondisclosable as a lobbying expenditure.³

     We appreciate your assistance with our request.  If you need
any further information, please do not hesitate to call me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        James R. Sutton

JRS/sfr




_______________________
³The salaries will of course be disclosed by the public
official on his or her Statement of Economic Interests.

Jeanne Pritchard  
February 11, 1991  

Statements of Economic Interests? If disclosable, are the costs properly characterized as "income" or "gifts"?

7. Must the firm report the salaries or other income of our public official/attorneys on its lobbying firm report as an activity expense or elsewhere?

In addition, we understand that if a partner or the spouse of a partner becomes a public official and the partner owns 10% or more of the firm, the public official will also disclose the identity of all clients of the firm who pay the firm fees rendering that partner's share $10,000 or more

1All further statutory references are to the Government Code.

Jeanne Pritchard  
February 11, 1991  

($20,000 if the spouse is the public official). (§§ 82030, 87207(b)(2).)

In addition to receiving compensation, our attorneys periodically attend firm-related functions, some of which entail travel to and accommodation in other cities. Spouses sometimes accompany attorneys to these functions. The firm usually pays for both attorneys' and their spouses' expenses, including travel, hotel and meal costs.

sections.analysis:
Questions 1 to 3. Based on your previous advice to Roger Brown (No. I-89-602 (11/28/89)), we understand that the Commission's position is that the firm will not be making a "gift" to the public official/spouse by paying for his or her attendance at and travel to firm-related functions. According to the Brown letter, the expense payment to the public official/spouse will not violate section 86203 and will not be reportable by the public official/spouse or the firm.2

Questions 4 and 5. The conclusions of the Brown letter also seem to apply to the situation in which a client invites the firm's attorneys to a social event, tells them they may bring a guest of their choosing, and then reimburses the firm for their expenses. Even if an attorney brings a public official, neither the firm nor the client intends the payment of the public official's expenses to be a gift to the public official/spouse. The Act's reporting and limitation rules therefore do not seem to apply.

2The Brown letter concerned a law firm (which also was a lobbying firm) which had several employees whose spouses were public officials. You advised the firm that its employees could bring their spouses who were public officials to firm events, without violating the ten dollar gift limit, as long as the firm neither "invited, suggested nor earmarked an invitation" for the spouses. (See also Advice Letter No. A-85-126 to Gordon Duffy (6/27/85).) In addition, you advised the firm that the payments were not disclosable on the firm's lobbying firm report as long as the firm did not intend them to be gifts to the public officials/spouses.

The facts of our situation appear very similar to the facts of the Brown letter. Our firm does not require attorneys to bring spouses to firm-related events but does indicate that they may bring a guest of their choosing. In addition, when the firm pays the spouse's expenses and travel costs to a firm-related event, it does not intend the payment to be a gift to a public official since the spouse's public appointment is irrelevant to the firm.

Question 6. Your previous advice to Daniel Pellissier (No. A-87-280 (12/8/87)) suggests that attorneys who become public officials will not be required to disclose the costs of firm-related or client-initiated events on their Statements of Economic Interests. The Pellissier letter concluded that ". . . in computing 'income,' officials are not required to include health benefits and other routine employment benefits which are provided by the employee to all employee." Firm lunches, parties and other social events seem to fall within this exception to the definition of income -- because they are "routine" and because they are provided, in different degrees, to all employees. The letter also concluded that the public benefit from reporting routine fringe benefits is outweighed by the significant burden the reporting places on filers. This rationale for nondisclosure applies equally to public officials reporting the costs of meals and parties provided through their employment.

Question 7. In the Morrissey Opinion (2 FPPC Opns. 84 (7/6/76)), the Commission concluded that a lobbyist employer need not disclose the salary of an employee who sits on an advisory committee of a local agency as an "other payment to influence administrative or legislative action" on its lobbyist employer report. The Commission based its conclusion on the fact that the employee did not sit on the committee to serve the interest of his employer. (2 FPPC Opns. at p. 89.)

Based on the Morrissey opinion, you advised Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro, a law firm with reporting obligations as a lobbying firm, that it must disclose on its lobbying firm report payments it receives from clients for work one of its attorneys does as a member of a state commission. (Advice Letter No. A-86-284 to Teresa Craigie (10/31/86).) Because the firm billed its clients for the attorney's time on the commission, you concluded that he was acting to represent the interests of the client and the firm.

In our case, the status of our attorneys and/or their spouses who may be appointed to boards or commissions resembles the status of the employee in the Morrissey opinion and is distinguishable from the attorney in the Craigie advice letter. As stated above, our attorneys will decide whether to accept appointments on their own, will serve in their capacity as private citizens, and will not represent the interests of the firm or its clients. Also, the firm will not bill any of its clients for attorneys' work on boards or commissions. The

Jeanne Pritchard  
February 11, 1991  

salaries of the public officials/attorneys therefore seem nondisclosable as a lobbying expenditure.3

We appreciate your assistance with our request. If you need any further information, please do not hesitate to call me.
